original_abstract: The cultivation of piety (*taqwa*) serves as a foundational expression of faith, refining the character of believers and guiding them toward a righteous life. According to the Qur'an, *taqwa* represents an internal orientation toward Allah that manifests as submission and obedience to divine teachings, characterized by the fulfillment of His commands and the avoidance of His prohibitions. This research explores the methodology for achieving such devotion through the lens of Buya Hamka’s seminal work, *Tafsir al-Azhar*. Utilizing a qualitative, library-based research design, this study draws primarily from *Tafsir al-Azhar*, supplemented by relevant books, journals, and scholarly works. The findings reveal that, for Hamka, *taqwa* extends beyond a mere protective measure against divine punishment; it encompasses a vibrant spirit of worship, the consistent performance of righteous deeds to draw closer to the Creator, and the propagation of compassion and social responsibility. Ultimately, the implementation of piety is bifurcated into two essential dimensions: *hablun min Allah* (the preservation of a vertical relationship with God through obedience) and *hablun min al-nas* (the maintenance of horizontal relationships characterized by empathy and care for fellow human beings).
humanized_abstract: Taqwa (cultivating piety) is seen as a central attribute of a Muslim, a faith that then develops into moral characteristics. According to the Quran taqwa is seen as the believer’s attitude towards Allah; their “heart attitude” toward Allah characterised by obedience to Allah’s commandments and shunning Allah’s prohibitions. This study aims at knowing how the attitude of taqwa can be actualised based on the Islamic exegesis from Buya Hamka’s works entitled Tafsir al-Azhar. Based on the qualitative descriptive library research, this study made an in-depth analysis based on Hamka’s exposition of Quranic verse in Tafsir al-Azhar and was complemented and reinforced by additional readings from other religious texts, journals and academic studies. The study revealed that for Buya Hamka taqwa refers to the spirit of worship; fulfilling the obligatory deeds to increase their mutual relationship with Allah as well as disseminating the spirits of brotherhood and humanity. Hence, based on the theory of Hamka it can be concluded that the realisation of taqwa is consists of two parts of obligations namely vertical obligation or hablun min Allah which is the believer’s obligations in establishing relation towards Allah; and the horizontal obligations or hablun min al-nas which is the believers obligations in socialising or interacting each other as human being.
